1: The wilderness generation as our fathers: Paul's inclusion of Gentile Corinthians in Israel's story is striking. By calling the Exodus generation our fathers, he adopts Gentile believers into the narrative of Israel — a move that reflects his understanding of the church as the eschatological Israel.
2: Baptism into Moses: the analogy is typological, not sacramental identity. The cloud (Exodus 13:21; Numbers 14:14) and the sea (Exodus 14:22) functioned as Israel's initiation into the Mosaic covenant — structurally parallel to the church's water baptism into Christ.
3: Spiritual food (broma pneumatikon): the manna of Exodus 16 is reread as pneumatikon — Spirit-bearing, having a deeper reality that points beyond itself. Paul applies a hermeneutic he learned from Jewish scripture interpretation (compare Wisdom of Solomon 1 Corinthians 16:20-21, which calls manna the food of angels).
4: The rock that followed: a rabbinic tradition (Tosefta Sukkah 1 Corinthians 3:11; Pseudo-Philo Biblical Antiquities 1 Corinthians 10:7) held that the water-bearing rock of Numbers 20 miraculously traveled with Israel through the wilderness. Paul adopts this tradition and identifies the rock as Christ — a pre-incarnate divine presence in Israel's story.
5: The majority perished: of the adult males who left Egypt in the Exodus, only Joshua and Caleb entered Canaan (Numbers 14:29-30). The word katastrephō (scatter, overthrow) echoes the LXX language of divine judgment on Israel's rebellions.
6: Typoi (patterns/types): the term is key to Paul's exegesis. In Hellenistic rhetorical culture, typos meant a pattern, impression, or mold. Paul applies it to the Exodus events as divinely designed prefigurations whose full meaning appears only in the church age.
7: The golden calf — Exodus 32:6: Paul cites the LXX exactly. The Israelite feast at Sinai combined eating, drinking, and pagan revelry (paizein, to play/sport, often with sexual connotation in LXX). The explicit parallel to Corinthian idol-feast participation is intentional.
8: The Baal Peor incident (Numbers 25): the 23,000 figure (vs. 24,000 in Numbers 25:9) may reflect a different counting tradition or a specific sub-event. The Phinehas story in Numbers 25 connects sexual immorality and idolatry explicitly — precisely the combination Paul is addressing in chapters 5-10.
9: Testing Christ in the wilderness: the serpent plague of Numbers 21:5-6 arose when Israel spoke against God and Moses. Paul's identification of the pre-incarnate Christ as the one being tested links his christology of Christ's wilderness presence (1 Corinthians 10:4) to the warning.
10: The Destroyer (olothreutēs): the term appears in Exodus 12:23 for the destroying angel of the Passover. Numbers 14:37 and 16:49 record plague-deaths connected to Israel's murmuring. The figure of the Destroyer appears also in Jewish apocalyptic (Jubilees 1 Corinthians 49:2; Wisdom 1 Corinthians 18:25).
11: The ends of the ages (ta telē tōn aiōnōn): Paul's eschatological self-location. Jewish apocalyptic divided history into ages (olam, aiōn); the messianic age was the telos (end-point and goal) of the earlier ages. Paul places the church at the hinge-point where the ages converge.
12: The danger of self-confidence: in Jewish wisdom tradition (Proverbs 16:18; Sir 1 Corinthians 32:21), overconfidence precedes the fall. The Corinthians who pride themselves on their gnōsis are exhibiting precisely the arrogance the wisdom tradition identifies as the condition for disaster.
13: Temptation common to humanity (anthropinos): Paul assures them that their testing is not superhuman — it belongs to the human condition. The phrase echoes philosophical discussions of human endurance (metriopatheia in Stoicism) and Jewish prayer for not being led into testing (cf. the Lord's Prayer).
14: Flee idolatry (pheugete eidōlolatrian): the verb pheugō (flee) appears in Jewish moral exhortation for sexual immorality (Genesis 39:12; Joseph flees Potiphar's wife) and in NT ethical teaching (2 Timothy 2:22). The urgency of flight rather than negotiation reflects the zero-tolerance stance of Jewish monotheism toward idol-worship.
15: Addressing them as sensible (phronimoi): the word phronimos (prudent, sensible, wise) was a term of honor in Greek culture — the phronimos was the person whose good judgment could be trusted. Paul uses the term with mild irony given the Corinthians' self-regard as the wise ones (sophoi) of chapter 1.
16: The cup of blessing — Passover context: the phrase potērion tēs eulogias (cup of blessing) is the Aramaic-Hebrew kos shel berakha, the third cup of the Passover Seder, blessed and shared after the main meal. Paul's Last Supper liturgy emerges from the Passover setting.
17: One bread, one body: in Hellenistic club-culture (thiasoi, collegia), sharing a common meal created social solidarity. Paul intensifies this: the shared loaf does not merely symbolize unity but constitutes the body. The bread is not a sign pointing elsewhere — it is the thing itself.
18: Israel according to the flesh: the phrase kata sarka (according to the flesh) distinguishes from Israel according to the Spirit (Romans 9:6-8). Paul draws on actual temple practice: Leviticus 7:14-18 shows that eating the peace offering created a bond with the altar and hence with Israel's God.
19: Idol as nothing: the standard Jewish polemic against idols is developed at length in Isaiah 44:9-20, Psalms 115:4-8, and Wisdom of Solomon 13-15. Paul stands in this tradition: the idol has no ontological reality as a divine being.
20: Demons behind the idols: Deuteronomy 32:17 (they sacrificed to demons, not to God) is the scriptural basis. Jubilees 22:17 and 1 Enoch 99:7 similarly identify idol-worship with demon-worship. Paul is not introducing a new idea but drawing on established Jewish demonology.
21: Two tables — the tablecloth of the Lord: the phrase trapeza kyriou (table of the Lord) appears in Malachi 1:7, 12 for the altar of the Jerusalem temple. Paul transfers it to the Eucharistic meal, creating an implicit argument: just as Jews cannot eat at the Lord's altar and at a foreign altar, so Christians cannot eat at the Lord's table and at a demon's table.
22: Provoking the Lord's jealousy: Deuteronomy 32:21 (the Song of Moses) — they provoked me with what is not God. The divine jealousy (qin'ah/zelos) is the response of the covenant God to Israel's idolatry throughout the Deuteronomistic history. Paul applies this covenant category to the church.
23: The Corinthian slogan (all things are permissible): this appears to be a phrase some Corinthians used, possibly derived from Pauline preaching about freedom from Torah-regulations. Paul quotes it back and qualifies it with the criteria of benefit (sympherei) and edification (oikodomei).
24: The neighbor-principle: Paul's ethical reorientation from individual rights to communal benefit echoes the love-commandment (Leviticus 19:18; Matthew 22:39). The neighbor (plesion) in Jewish usage meant primarily the fellow member of the covenant community.
25: The macellum (meat market): the Corinthian agora had a macellum (meat market — confirmed by archaeological inscription from Corinth reading MACELLVM). Meat sold there could have come from temple sacrifice, from private slaughter, or from public festivals. Paul advises not to investigate provenance — buy and eat without scrupulous questioning.
26: Psalms 24:1 (the earth is the Lord's): the Psalm was used in Jewish table liturgy — the Talmud (b. Berakhot 35a) records that this verse was recited before eating as acknowledgment that all food belongs to God. Paul invokes the same liturgical tradition to ground his practical advice.
27: Dining with unbelievers: private dinner invitations were a major mechanism of social networking in Roman Corinth. To refuse all invitations from unbelievers would effectively withdraw from civic life. Paul's advice to attend and eat freely maintains the church's social engagement.
28: Hierothyton (pagan term for idol meat): the unbeliever uses the pagan term hierothyton (sacrificed to a sacred place) rather than Paul's polemical eidōlothyton (sacrificed to an idol). Paul switches to the pagan's own vocabulary, showing sensitivity to the social context of the dinner.
29: The conscience of the other: Paul's distinction between one's own conscience and the other's conscience draws on Stoic moral psychology, where syneidesis (conscience) was the internal faculty that judges one's own actions and renders verdicts. Paul makes it relational rather than merely individual.
30: Eating with thanksgiving (eucharistia): the practice of giving thanks before meals was standard in both Jewish (birkat ha-mazon, the blessing after meals) and early Christian communities. The Didache (1 Corinthians 9:1-3) preserves early eucharistic prayers that echo this thanksgiving tradition.
31: All to the glory of God: the comprehensive formula eis doxan theou (to the glory of God) appears in Jewish liturgical contexts (the Kaddish praises God's name) and in early Christian doxologies. Paul makes it the regulating principle for all conduct, including eating and drinking.
32: Three communities — Jews, Greeks, church: this tripartite division is characteristic of Paul's social world-view. The church is neither Jewish nor Greek but a new community created by the gospel — what he calls the new creation (2 Corinthians 5:17; Galatians 6:15) and elsewhere the one new man (Ephesians 2:15).
33: Pleasing all people (areskein pasin): the goal of pleasing all people was a philosophical concept in Hellenistic ethics — the Stoics distinguished between proper and improper forms of people-pleasing. Paul's formulation avoids sycophancy by anchoring it in the salvation of the many rather than personal advancement.
